title: PIC — Causal Authority Transition
description: The core PIC protocol. An executor E_i holding PCA_i wants to delegate restricted authority to the next executor E_{i+1}. It cannot simply forward PCA_i — PCA is bound to E_i and non-transferable. Instead, E_i requests a freshness challenge (PCC) from the Trust Plane (CAT), constructs a Proof of Continuity (PoC) demonstrating both authority over the predecessor PCA and possession of an attested executor identity, and the CAT mints a new PCA_{i+1} that strictly restricts ops (ops_{i+1} ⊆ ops_i) and is bound to E_{i+1}. p_0 is preserved verbatim. This single mechanism repeats at every hop in a PIC transaction, building the causal chain. References pic-spec.md §4.3 (CAT verification), §5.4 (request binding).

steps.label: GET /pcc (request challenge)
steps.explanation: E_i is about to delegate to E_{i+1}. Before signing a PoC, it asks the CAT for a fresh challenge (PCC). The challenge prevents replay: a PoC bound to PCC_{i+1} cannot be reused at any other hop or against any other CAT. E_i declares (informationally) the predecessor hop number and the intended successor executor — these will be re-verified inside the PoC itself.
annotations: The challenge is the freshness mechanism. Without it, a captured PoC could be replayed by an adversary who later compromised E_i's key.
PCC issuance is cheap (random nonce + bookkeeping). It is the validation work in step 3 that is load-bearing.
Some embedded-CAT deployments skip the round-trip and use a deterministic challenge derived from a shared monotonic counter. The security property (one-time-use) must still hold.

steps.label: PCC_{i+1} (challenge)

artifacts.raw_input:
Random source: CSPRNG (256 bits)
Transaction ID: txn-7d9a2e
Successor target: spiffe://example.com/svc/order-processor
artifacts.signing_key_hint: No signing — PCC is a one-time-use nonce, not a credential.

steps.label: 1. Generate freshness nonce
steps.value:
challenge = CSPRNG(256 bits)
         = 0x9a7e2f1b8c4d6a3e5f0192abcd76ef83

The CAT records (challenge, predecessor_hop,
successor_target, valid_until) in its short-lived
challenge store. When the matching PoC arrives, the
CAT MUST find this entry, MUST mark it consumed, and
MUST reject any second use.

60-second window is conservative; some deployments
use 5-15 seconds for high-throughput services.

steps.label: 2. Why a server-issued challenge (not just timestamp)
steps.value:
A naive design would let E_i pick its own jti+iat
and let CAT validate freshness from a clock.
Problems:
  ✗ Clock skew tolerance widens replay window
  ✗ Adversary with key + recent jti corpus can replay
  ✗ No causal binding to specific successor

Server-issued PCC fixes all three:
  ✓ Freshness tied to CAT's own state, not clocks
  ✓ One-shot consumption — no re-use ever
  ✓ Bound to (predecessor_hop, successor_target),
    so a PoC for one hop cannot be replayed at another

steps.explanation: The CAT issues PCC_{i+1} — a 256-bit random nonce paired with context (the CAT's own kid, the predecessor hop number, a short validity window). The challenge is bound to E_i's intended successor, so a PoC built around it can only be used to mint PCA for that specific transition.
annotations: PCC is not a credential. It carries no authority on its own and is not signed.
The CAT MUST cache (challenge, consumed?) until valid_until expires. Garbage-collected after that.
If the CAT is sharded/HA, the challenge store needs cross-shard consistency — typically a fast KV store with short TTL keys.

steps.label: POST /poc (submit PoC_{i+1})

artifacts.raw_input:
Inputs to the PoC construction:
  predecessor PCA_i = (hop=5, p_0=alice, ops=(read:orders/*, read:catalog/*, write:orders/*))
  successor proposal:
    ops_{i+1} = (read:orders/*)            ← strict subset of ops_i
    executor = spiffe://example.com/svc/order-processor
    constraints.exp = predecessor.exp − Δ   ← never extend
  attestations = [SPIFFE SVID for E_i]
  challenge = 0x9a7e2f1b8c4d6a3e5f0192abcd76ef83  ← from PCC
artifacts.signing_key_hint: E_i's executor private key (paired with the SPIFFE SVID public key in the attestation)
artifacts.final_output:
PoC_{i+1} = COSE_Sign1(
  protected_header = {alg, kid, challenge},
  payload          = {predecessor, successor, attestations[]},
  signature        = Ed25519(E_i_private, header || payload)
)

steps.label: 1. Protected header — bind to PCC
steps.value:
{
  "alg": "EdDSA",
  "kid": "spiffe://example.com/svc/checkout#key-1",
  "challenge": "0x9a7e2f1b8c4d6a3e5f0192abcd76ef83"
}

challenge in the protected header binds this PoC to the
specific PCC issued in step 2. If the CAT does not find
this exact challenge in its one-time-use store, validation
fails — replay is impossible.

kid is the executor's public key identifier, resolvable
via E_i's attestation. This key is NOT a CAT-issued
credential — it is E_i's own identity key.

steps.label: 2. Payload — predecessor + successor proposal
3. Per-attestation PoP — context binding
steps.value:
Each attestation carries its own PoP signature,
which proves possession of the attestation's key AND
binds the attestation to THIS specific PoC's context.

PoP_input = SHA-256(protected_header || payload)
PoP       = Sign(attestation_private_key, PoP_input)

Why two signatures (PoC outer + PoP per attestation)?

Without the per-attestation PoP, a captured SVID +
leaked PoC body could be re-used by stitching together
a new PoC that re-binds the same SVID to a different
context. The PoP forces every attestation usage to be
cryptographically tied to the hash of the PoC payload
it appears in — no rebinding possible.

This is the same property DPoP achieves for OAuth
sender-constraint, applied per-attestation here.

steps.explanation: E_i constructs PoC_{i+1} — a COSE_Sign1 envelope carrying the predecessor PCA_i in full, a strictly-narrower successor proposal, and an executor attestation with its own PoP. The challenge from PCC_{i+1} appears in the protected header, binding this PoC to that specific issuance moment. The whole envelope is signed with E_i's executor private key.
annotations: The successor proposal is a PROPOSAL — the CAT may still reject it (out-of-policy ops, expired predecessor, attestation revoked, failed monotonicity check).
Including the full predecessor PCA in the payload means the CAT can validate without state lookups. This is how PIC scales horizontally.
Multiple attestations are allowed (e.g., SPIFFE SVID + TEE quote + VP) when the executor needs to demonstrate multiple identity facets. Each attestation carries its own PoP.

steps.label: PCA_{i+1} (signed)

artifacts.raw_input:
CAT validation pipeline before signing PCA_{i+1}

PoC from step 3:
  challenge = 0x9a7e2f1b8c4d6a3e5f0192abcd76ef83
  predecessor.ops = (read:orders/*, read:catalog/*, write:orders/*)
  successor.ops   = (read:orders/*)
  predecessor.p_0 = alice@example.com
  attestation     = SPIFFE SVID for checkout

CAT trust anchors:
  Fed Bridge key (for PCA_0 lineage) ✓
  SPIRE trust bundle (for SVID validation) ✓
  PCC store (one-time-use challenges) ✓
artifacts.signing_key_hint: CAT's signing key (kid: cat-kid-2026-01) — used AFTER all checks pass
artifacts.final_output: All 6 checks PASS → CAT signs PCA_{i+1}

steps.label: 1. Verify PoC outer signature
steps.value:
Extract kid from protected header.
Resolve public key via the SVID in the attestations
array (SVID's X.509 certificate carries the public key).

verify_signature(
  PoC_payload || PoC_protected_header,
  PoC_signature,
  SVID.public_key
) → MUST be valid

FAIL → reject. The signer is not who they claim.

steps.label: 2. Verify each attestation + PoP
steps.value:
For each attestation in attestations[]:

  a) Validate the attestation against its issuer:
     SPIFFE SVID → SPIRE trust bundle
     TEE quote   → vendor attestation service
     X.509 cert  → CA chain
     VP          → DID resolution + VC issuer keys

  b) Verify per-attestation PoP:
     PoP_input = SHA-256(protected_header || payload)
     verify_signature(
       PoP_input,
       attestation.pop,
       attestation.public_key
     ) → MUST be valid

This prevents rebinding a captured SVID into a different
PoC. Each attestation is cryptographically glued to
exactly this PoC's payload hash (pic-spec.md §4.3).

steps.label: 3. Verify predecessor PCA_i + consume challenge
steps.value:
Verify PCA_i signature against known trust anchor:
  PCA_i.kid → Fed Bridge or prior CAT → public key
  verify_signature(PCA_i_payload, PCA_i_sig, pubkey) ✓

Consume PCC challenge (atomic):
  lookup challenge 0x9a7e2f1b8c4d6a3e5f0192abcd76ef83
    → EXISTS ✓  NOT consumed ✓  NOT expired ✓
    → matches (predecessor_hop=5, successor_target) ✓
  MARK consumed = true

steps.label: 4. Enforce monotonicity (the core invariant)
steps.value:
ops_{i+1}     = ["read:orders/*"]
ops_i (pred)  = ["read:orders/*", "read:catalog/*",
                 "write:orders/*"]

Check:  ops_{i+1} ⊆ ops_i

For each op in ops_{i+1}:
  read:orders/* ∈ ops_i? → YES ✓

→ subset proven

Constraints:
  successor.exp = 1770000120
  predecessor.exp = 1770003605
  1770000120 ≤ 1770003605 ✓

Origin immutability:
  PCA_{i+1}.p_0 will carry alice@example.com
  verbatim — the CAT cannot be told to change it.

All invariants hold → mint PCA_{i+1}.

steps.explanation: After all 6 checks pass, the CAT mints PCA_{i+1}: hop incremented to 6, p_0 carried forward verbatim as alice@example.com, ops strictly narrower at (read:orders/*), executor bound to E_{i+1}, and provenance back-referencing both the predecessor CAT signature and E_i's PoC signature. The new exp (1770000120) is much shorter than the predecessor's.
annotations: provenance.executor.sig captures that E_i (specifically) authorized this transition — audit can verify who signed what years later.
PCA_{i+1}.executor.binding is the NEXT executor (E_{i+1}); the attestations in PoC were E_i's. This handoff is the binding chain.
CAT key rotation: overlap windows, sign-with-old/verify-with-both during cutover. The kid in the header is what downstream verifiers resolve.

steps.label: Forward request + PCA_{i+1}

artifacts.signing_key_hint: E_i's executor private key (same key kid as in PoC_{i+1})

steps.label: 1. Why also sign the request body (pic-spec.md §5.4)
steps.value:
PCA_{i+1} is bound to E_{i+1} (the addressee).
It does NOT prove the attached request body was sent
by E_i. An on-path adversary could swap the body while
keeping PCA_{i+1} intact.

E_i signs (request_body || PCA_{i+1}) with the SAME key
it used for PoC_{i+1}. E_{i+1} verifies using the public
key referenced in PCA_{i+1}.provenance.executor.kid.

This closes the loop: body is cryptographically bound
to the specific E_i that created this transition.

Prevents:
  ✗ Body tampering on transit
  ✗ Replay against a different executor
  ✗ Substitution of a stale or forged request

steps.explanation: E_i forwards the actual business request to E_{i+1}, attaching PCA_{i+1} and a request-body signature using the same key it signed PoC with. Without the request signature, PCA would be a bearer token bound only to recipient identity — PIC also binds the request body itself to E_i's authoring (pic-spec.md §5.4).
annotations: The transport is HTTP here for familiarity. PIC is transport-agnostic — gRPC, queue messages, MQTT all work as long as PCA + request signature can travel together.
If E_i needs to fan out the same operation to multiple downstream services, it MUST get a fresh PCA per recipient (each bound to that recipient's executor identity).
X-PIC-PCA and X-PIC-Request-Sig are illustrative header names. Real deployments may use a single structured envelope or a sidecar metadata channel.

steps.label: 200 OK (restricted ops enforced)
steps.explanation: E_{i+1} validates PCA_{i+1} (signature against CAT's public key, executor.binding matches its own identity, ops and p_0 legible) and the request signature (against E_i's key from provenance). All checks pass; it executes the read under ops_{i+1} = (read:orders/*). Even if E_{i+1} were compromised and tried to write, ops does not include write — any downstream call attempting write would fail at the next CAT.
annotations: The audit envelope shows what PIC makes verifiable: this read happened under alice's authority, with exactly ops (read:orders/*), at hop 6.
If E_{i+1} needs to call E_{i+2}, it repeats this entire flow as the new E_i. The chain extends indefinitely; security argument is identical at every hop.
Confused deputy is not formulable: even if a tenant tricked E_{i+1} into making an unintended call, that call carries alice's ops only — not E_{i+1}'s ambient privileges, because there are none.
